verses.text: Abimelech son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying, Speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you? remember also that I am your bone and your flesh. The men of Shechem spake all these words in his ears: and their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother. Guidance: Abimelech opens by exploiting two angles: tribal loyalty ('your bone and your flesh' — Shechem is his mother's hometown) and a false dilemma ('70 lords or 1 king'). His brothers were not threatening to reign; he invented the choice. Manipulation often starts by simplifying real life into a binary. Shechem buys it because he is theirs — a half-Shechemite king is more flattering to local pride than a Manassite leader from Ophrah. Identity politics displaces theology in one paragraph.

They gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baal-berith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him. He went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself. All the men of Shechem gathered together, and all the house of Millo, and went, and made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem. Guidance: Seventy pieces of silver from the temple of Baal-berith — one shekel per brother to be murdered. The math is deliberate. The Baal Gideon failed to keep buried funds the slaughter of Gideon's sons. Idolatry's bill comes due in the second generation. 'Upon one stone' — a single execution block. Only Jotham, the youngest, hides. They crown Abimelech 'by the pillar' at Shechem — perhaps the same stone Joshua set as covenant witness in Joshua 24:26. The covenant stone witnesses a coronation in defiance of covenant. The most sacred sites are sometimes the saddest.

When they told it to Jotham, he went and stood in the top of mount Gerizim, and lifted up his voice, and cried, and said unto them, Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you. The trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us. But the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness, wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees? The trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us. But the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit, and go to be promoted over the trees? Then said the trees unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us. The vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees? Then said all the trees unto the bramble, Come thou, and reign over us. And the bramble said unto the trees, If in truth ye anoint me king over you, then come and put your trust in my shadow: and if not, let fire come out of the bramble, and devour the cedars of Lebanon. Guidance: Jotham stands on Gerizim — the blessings-mountain of Deut 11 — and delivers Scripture's first political fable. Three useful trees decline royalty because they have real work to do — olive (oil for anointing kings and lamps), fig (sweetness), vine (wine). The bramble, useless except for sparking wildfires, accepts. The image is devastating: a fruitless thornbush that produces no shade demands that everyone shelter under it, on threat of burning. Abimelech is the bramble. Worthless leaders gather power; productive leaders decline it. The fable predicts how Abimelech will eventually destroy the very people who chose him — the bramble's fire will reach the cedars.

Now therefore, if ye have done truly and sincerely, in that ye have made Abimelech king, and if ye have dealt well with Jerubbaal and his house, and have done unto him according to the deserving of his hands; (for my father fought for you, and adventured his life far, and delivered you out of the hand of Midian: and ye are risen up against my father's house this day, and have slain his sons, threescore and ten persons, upon one stone, and have made Abimelech, the son of his maidservant, king over the men of Shechem, because he is your brother;) if ye then have dealt truly and sincerely with Jerubbaal and with his house this day, then rejoice ye in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you: but if not, let fire come out of Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem, and the house of Millo; and let fire come out from the men of Shechem, and from the house of Millo, and devour Abimelech. Jotham ran away, and fled, and went to Beer, and dwelt there, for fear of Abimelech his brother. Guidance: Jotham applies the fable. If you really treated my father's house fairly, then enjoy your bramble-king. If not — and you didn't — then may the fire from this fake king and the fire from his city consume each other. It is a self-fulfilling curse, sealed by truth. Then Jotham runs. Speaking prophecy from a mountaintop and then living to see it fulfilled requires two skills: courage to climb and prudence to flee. He does both.

He does both.

When Abimelech had reigned three years over Israel, then God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem; and the men of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech: that the cruelty done to the threescore and ten sons of Jerubbaal might come, and their blood be laid upon Abimelech their brother, which slew them; and upon the men of Shechem, which aided him in the killing of his brethren. The men of Shechem set liers in wait for him in the top of the mountains, and they robbed all that came along that way by them: and it was told Abimelech. Guidance: 'God sent an evil spirit' — the same language as 1 Sam 16 with Saul. God breaks up the unholy alliance He had permitted them to form. The shared crime no longer holds them together; mutual suspicion takes its place. Shechem starts robbing travelers on roads that Abimelech's government can't keep open. The economy collapses; the king's enemies are his own constituents. Even kings without God lose what they took without God.

Gaal son of Ebed came with his brethren, and went over to Shechem: and the men of Shechem put their confidence in him. They went out into the fields, and gathered their vineyards, and trode the grapes, and made merry, and went into the house of their god, and did eat and drink, and cursed Abimelech. Gaal son of Ebed said, Who is Abimelech, and who is Shechem, that we should serve him? is not he the son of Jerubbaal? and Zebul his officer? serve the men of Hamor the father of Shechem: for why should we serve him? Guidance: Gaal arrives just in time to be the city's drunken hero. After grape-harvest festival, in the temple of Baal-berith, the wine flows and so do the curses on Abimelech. Gaal makes the old appeal: Abimelech is foreign-blood (Jerubbaal's son), come on, serve your real founders. The chapter that started with manipulating Shechemite pride against Gideon's family now sees the same pride manipulated against Abimelech. Cities that elevate identity over covenant find every new leader becomes the wrong identity.

When Zebul the ruler of the city heard the words of Gaal son of Ebed, his anger was kindled. He sent messengers unto Abimelech privily, saying, Behold, Gaal son of Ebed and his brethren be come to Shechem; and, behold, they fortify the city against thee. Now therefore up by night, thou and the people that is with thee, and lie in wait in the field: it shall be, that in the morning, as soon as the sun is up, thou shalt rise early, and set upon the city: and, behold, when he and the people that is with him come out against thee, then mayest thou do to them as thou shalt find occasion. Guidance: Zebul — Abimelech's officer left in Shechem — sends private messengers. Treachery against treachery; the city is now full of plots. He gives precise tactical advice: night ambush, morning attack, kill them as they come out for the day's work. The narrative is amoral on purpose; it documents the spiral. Once you have broken covenant for power, every alliance becomes brittle and every neighbor becomes an asset to be used or a threat to be removed.

Abimelech rose up, and all the people that were with him, by night, and they laid wait against Shechem in four companies. Gaal son of Ebed went out, and stood in the entering of the gate of the city: and Abimelech rose up, and the people that were with him, from lying in wait. When Gaal saw the people, he said to Zebul, Behold, there come people down from the top of the mountains. Zebul said unto him, Thou seest the shadow of the mountains as if they were men. Gaal spake again and said, See there come people down by the middle of the land, and another company come along by the plain of Meonenim. Then said Zebul unto him, Where is now thy mouth, wherewith thou saidst, Who is Abimelech, that we should serve him? is not this the people that thou hast despised? go out, I pray now, and fight with them. Gaal went out before the men of Shechem, and fought with Abimelech. Abimelech chased him, and he fled before him, and many were overthrown and wounded, even unto the entering of the gate. Abimelech dwelt at Arumah: and Zebul thrust out Gaal and his brethren, that they should not dwell in Shechem. Guidance: Gaal stands at the gate looking out and Zebul, knowing what's coming, plays innocent: 'You're seeing shadows.' He drags the conversation out until Abimelech's forces are too close to escape, then pivots: 'Where's that loud mouth now? Go fight.' Gaal does fight, and loses badly. The drunken boast of the previous chapter becomes the slaughter of the morning. The narrative does not pity Gaal; pride that ferments overnight pays the bill at dawn. Zebul evicts him; loyalty in court politics has a short half-life.

On the morrow the people went out into the field; and they told Abimelech. He took the people, and divided them into three companies, and laid wait in the field, and looked, and, behold, the people were come forth out of the city; he rose up against them, and smote them. Abimelech, and the company that was with him, rushed forward, and stood in the entering of the gate of the city: and the two other companies ran upon all the people that were in the fields, and slew them. Abimelech fought against the city all that day; and he took the city, and slew the people that was therein, and beat down the city, and sowed it with salt. Guidance: Abimelech destroys the very city that crowned him. He takes the gate (so they cannot retreat home), kills those in the fields, then kills the city itself. Then sows it with salt — a symbolic curse of permanent infertility. He razes the place his mother came from. The fire from the bramble has begun to reach the cedars. Power that comes by treachery turns its violence eventually on its own base of support.

When all the men of the tower of Shechem heard that, they entered into an hold of the house of the god Berith. It was told Abimelech, that all the men of the tower of Shechem were gathered together. Abimelech gat him up to mount Zalmon, he and all the people that were with him; and Abimelech took an axe in his hand, and cut down a bough from the trees, and took it, and laid it on his shoulder, and said unto the people that were with him, What ye have seen me do, make haste, and do as I have done. And all the people likewise cut down every man his bough, and followed Abimelech, and put them to the hold, and set the hold on fire upon them; so that all the men of the tower of Shechem died also, about a thousand men and women. Guidance: A thousand men and women take refuge in their god's temple. Baal-berith — 'Baal-of-the-covenant' — fails to keep covenant with them. Abimelech demonstrates by cutting his own bough, then echoes Gideon at the camp of Midian ('What ye see me do, make haste and do likewise') — but applies the same leadership move to atrocity instead of liberation. The same verbs that built a deliverer can build a butcher. The fire from the bramble reaches the temple of the false god. Jotham's curse fulfills its first half: fire from Abimelech consumes Shechem.

When all the men of the tower of Shechem heard that, they entered into an hold of the house of the god Berith. It was told Abimelech, that all the men of the tower of Shechem were gathered together. Abimelech gat him up to mount Zalmon, he and all the people that were with him; and Abimelech took an axe in his hand, and cut down a bough from the trees, and took it, and laid it on his shoulder, and said unto the people that were with him, What ye have seen me do, make haste, and do as I have done. And all the people likewise cut down every man his bough, and followed Abimelech, and put them to the hold, and set the hold on fire upon them; so that all the men of the tower of Shechem died also, about a thousand men and women. Guidance: A thousand men and women take refuge in their god's temple. Baal-berith — 'Baal-of-the-covenant' — fails to keep covenant with them. Abimelech demonstrates by cutting his own bough, then echoes Gideon at the camp of Midian ('What ye see me do, make haste and do likewise') — but applies the same leadership move to atrocity instead of liberation. The same verbs that built a deliverer can build a butcher. The fire from the bramble reaches the temple of the false god. Jotham's curse fulfills its first half: fire from Abimelech consumes Shechem.

Then went Abimelech to Thebez, and encamped against Thebez, and took it. But there was a strong tower within the city, and thither fled all the men and women, and all they of the city, and shut it to them, and gat them up to the top of the tower. Abimelech came unto the tower, and fought against it, and went hard unto the door of the tower to burn it with fire. A certain woman cast a piece of a millstone upon Abimelech's head, and all to brake his skull. Then he called hastily unto the young man his armourbearer, and said unto him, Draw thy sword, and slay me, that men say not of me, A woman slew him. His young man thrust him through, and he died. When the men of Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, they departed every man unto his place. Guidance: Abimelech rolls on to Thebez and tries the same playbook — torch the tower. An unnamed woman drops the upper millstone (the heavier of the two stones a household ran daily) on his head from the parapet. The skull cracks. His vanity even on the threshold of death is on display: 'don't let it be said a woman killed me.' The armorbearer obliges with a sword-thrust, but generations later in 2 Sam 11:21 it is exactly what gets said about him. The lie about how the king died does not outlive the truth; the woman gets her credit in the next book of the Bible. Pride dies louder than the body it inhabits.

Thus God rendered the wickedness of Abimelech, which he did unto his father, in slaying his seventy brethren: and all the evil of the men of Shechem did God render upon their heads: and upon them came the curse of Jotham son of Jerubbaal. Guidance: The narrator's verdict, twice in two verses: 'God rendered.' The chapter that has been a relentless tour of human treachery closes with one short sentence locating the true judge. Jotham's curse from the hilltop has been fulfilled to the letter — fire from Abimelech consumed Shechem, fire from Shechem consumed Abimelech. The God of the trees-fable has been the playwright all along. Sin's seasons are sometimes long; its accounting is exact.
